clinical_coherence.notes: 35M Hispanic with migraine history, known PFO, and recent cervical hyperextension while cycling. Presents with acute vertigo, diplopia, and left-sided cerebellar ataxia. Vitals normal (appropriate for young healthy patient). Neuro exam shows direction-changing nystagmus, left limb dysmetria, left Horner syndrome -- classic left PICA territory findings. Aspirin allergy adds management complexity.

tool_output_consistency.notes: Perfectly concordant. MRI shows left PICA territory cerebellar infarct with left vertebral artery V3 dissection (intramural hematoma on fat-sat T1). CTA confirms V3 dissection. ECG normal sinus (appropriate for young patient). Echo reveals PFO with atrial septal aneurysm. Hypercoagulability panel shows Factor V Leiden heterozygosity. All findings build a complex but coherent picture.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD I63.9. Diagnosis correctly identifies vertebral artery dissection as primary mechanism. Differential is excellent -- vestibular neuritis as initial concern, migraine with brainstem aura, MS, paradoxical embolism. Critical actions appropriately include HINTS exam, MRI with fat-sat T1, antithrombotic therapy, PFO evaluation, and cerebellar edema monitoring. tPA listed as relatively contraindicated in dissection.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Perfectly calibrated as diagnostic puzzle. Young patient without traditional risk factors presenting with vertigo that mimics peripheral vestibular disorder. Multiple competing mechanisms (dissection, PFO with thrombophilia, migraine history). Requires HINTS exam skills, recognition of central vestibular signs, and systematic young-stroke workup. Expected agent confidence 0.45 is appropriate.

demographic_plausibility.notes: Highly realistic. 35M physically active (cycling, basketball), Hispanic, healthy BMI. Migraine history and PFO both common in young adults. Father with DVT history (supports familial Factor V Leiden). The cervical hyperextension mechanism for vertebral dissection is a classic scenario. Aspirin allergy adds realistic complexity.

educational_value.notes: Exceptional teaching case. Covers HINTS exam for central vs peripheral vertigo, posterior fossa stroke recognition, vertebral artery dissection, PFO evaluation with bubble study, hypercoagulability workup, competing stroke mechanisms in young patients, and the critical point that CT misses ~40% of posterior fossa infarcts. Multiple high-yield clinical pearls.

strengths: Outstanding diagnostic puzzle with genuine ambiguity
Multiple competing mechanisms create real diagnostic challenge
HINTS exam teaching is clinically high-yield
Posterior circulation stroke recognition is critically important
